coredata.dc:title: Investigating the role of ultrasound in improving the photocatalytic ability of CQD decorated boron-doped g-C3N4 for tetracycline degradation and first-principles study of nitrogen-vacancy formation

coredata.dc:description: Graphitic carbon nitride (CN) is recognized as a promising photocatalyst for energy and environmental remediation. However, challenges persist due to limited CN surface-active sites and poor electronic properties. To overcome such challenges, we have synthesized Carbon Quantum Dots decorated Boron-doped Exfoliated CN (CQD@B-ECN) via ultrasound-assisted thermal polycondensation method. The substitutional boron doping favored nitrogen-vacancy as evident from experimental results and DFT calculations, which accelerated the charge separation/transfer and the CQD incorporation broadens the light-harvesting ability through photon upconversion. Moreover, the specific surface area of 2CQD-2B-ECN (685 m2/g) was significantly greater than CN providing enormous active sites. 2CQD-2B-ECN were evaluated using Tetracycline (TC) antibiotic, where photocatalysis and sonocatalysis showed 68% and 0.2% degradation in 120 min, respectively. Remarkably, sonophotocatalysis exhibited 84% TC degradation with a synergy index of 1.2 and complete degradation within 180 min. This work reveals an insight into CQD-B-ECN as a novel sonophotocatalyst for the degradation of diverse recalcitrant pollutants.

XPS spectra of the 2CQD-2B-ECN (a) survey, (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s and (e) B1s. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–vis-DRS spectra, (b) Tauc plots, (c) PL spectra, (d) TPC response, (e) EIS-Nyquist plots and (f) ESR spectra of CN, ECN, 2B-ECN, 2CQD-ECN and 2CQD-2B-ECN. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The proposed sonophotocatalytic degradation pathways of TC over 2CQD-2B-ECN.

head.ce:title: Investigating the role of ultrasound in improving the photocatalytic ability of CQD decorated boron-doped g-C3N4 for tetracycline degradation and first-principles study of nitrogen-vacancy formation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphitic carbon nitride (CN) is recognized as a promising photocatalyst for energy and environmental remediation. However, challenges persist due to limited CN surface-active sites and poor electronic properties. To overcome such challenges, we have synthesized Carbon Quantum Dots decorated Boron-doped Exfoliated CN (CQD@B-ECN) via ultrasound-assisted thermal polycondensation method. The substitutional boron doping favored nitrogen-vacancy as evident from experimental results and DFT calculations, which accelerated the charge separation/transfer and the CQD incorporation broadens the light-harvesting ability through photon upconversion. Moreover, the specific surface area of 2CQD-2B-ECN (685 m2/g) was significantly greater than CN providing enormous active sites. 2CQD-2B-ECN were evaluated using Tetracycline (TC) antibiotic, where photocatalysis and sonocatalysis showed 68% and 0.2% degradation in 120 min, respectively. Remarkably, sonophotocatalysis exhibited 84% TC degradation with a synergy index of 1.2 and complete degradation within 180 min. This work reveals an insight into CQD-B-ECN as a novel sonophotocatalyst for the degradation of diverse recalcitrant pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para: The widespread use of medications for human and veterinary wellbeing has raised environmental issues owing to the harmful chemical compound accumulation. Among various drug classes, antibiotics gained specific interest as they treat or prevent infectious diseases and promote growth rate in poultry and livestock production [1]. In particular, Tetracycline (TC) antibiotics are widely consumed because of their enhanced antibacterial activity by inhibiting protein synthesis, the minimum production cost with high-quality drugs and favorable oral administration [2]. However, TC absorption is challenging for human and animal bodies after administration because of its chemical stability. Consequently, the majority of maternal compounds are eliminated through the renal and biliary systems. Even after 69–86% of excretion from the bodies, a small fraction of metabolites is still biologically active [3]. Owing to such conditions, TC is detected in the ng/L-μg/L concentration range in an aqueous environment, i.e., surface water, groundwater, drinking water and wastewater. The occurrence of such compounds not only affects water quality but also threatens human health and the ecosystem. Besides, the residual TC promotes the development of drug-resistant genes and antibiotics resistant bacteria [4,5].

Therefore, the effective removal of such recalcitrant bio-accumulative compounds from the aqueous environment remains a big challenge. So far, several techniques have been utilized to treat the residual TC, namely adsorption, membrane processes, microbial degradation and advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) [6–8]. AOPs have gained much attention due to highly reactive radical technology as they oxidize recalcitrant, non-biodegradable and toxic components under ambient conditions. AOPs includes ultrasonication, heterogeneous photocatalysis, Fenton reaction, electrochemical and ozonation process [9–12]. To achieve effective degradation performance within a short duration, ultrasonic irradiation was performed in combination with light illumination, i.e., sonophotocatalysis. The utilization of ultrasonic irradiation improves the mass transfer by acoustic cavitation in aqueous solutions and thus increases surface-active sites in the photocatalyst [13–15]. Further, it promotes the reactive radical generation, de-aggregation of the particles, and overcome the mass transfer limitations. Therefore, sonophotocatalysis can synergistically enhance the degradation of recalcitrant bio-accumulative compounds as it eliminates the drawbacks associated with individual AOPs [16–20].

Recently, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has been established as one of the efficient photocatalysts as it shows higher activity when compared with commercial nitrogen-doped TiO2 for the degradation of organic pollutants [21]. Metal-free polymeric g-C3N4 (CN) offers immense potential because of its thermal and chemical stability along with simple production techniques. Though CN is structurally stable and shows good redox capability, it has some disadvantages such as low charge carrier mobility, moderate oxidation ability and insufficient sunlight absorption. Owing to such shortcomings in pristine CN, several modifications of CN including new synthesis approaches, nanostructure architecture, and electronic structure modulation have been conducted to optimize the photoactivity. Chemical doping of CN is considered as a simple and effective strategy to modify the surface and electronic properties along with the ionic conductivity of the semiconductor. The boron doping can generate Lewis acid sites on its surface and trap exciton dissociation [22–25]. Yan et al. constructed boron-doped CN where B was introduced into N sites through C-NB2 or 2C-NB groups [26]. Conversely, Wang et al. explored the C atoms substitution with B atoms to construct a π-bonded electronic configuration [27]. Nevertheless, theoretical evidence to validate the experimental doping site and explain the mechanism underlying these improved performances is inadequate.

Based on these findings, we postulate that the boron doping induced nitrogen-vacancy may improve the CN performance. Hence, DFT simulations are performed to demonstrate the theoretical possibility of this strategy. Furthermore, the incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can broaden the light-harvesting ability as well as transfer and store photo-generated electrons efficiently. Herein, CQD incorporated Boron-doped Exfoliated CN (CQD@B-ECN) were constructed by ultrasound-assisted thermal condensation. The synthesized catalysts were characterized and compared in terms of their morphologies and structures along with the optical and photoelectrochemical properties. The synergistic effect of CQD and B-ECN were evaluated by sonophotocatalytic removal of TC molecules (a model antibiotic contaminant). Lastly, a plausible sonophotocatalytic TC degradation mechanism has been proposed based on the electrochemical, scavenging and LC-MS data.

Melamine (C3H6N6) and tetracycline (C22H24N2O8) were acquired from Sigma Aldrich. Boric acid (H3BO3) was procured from SRL. Isopropyl alcohol (C3H8O) was obtained from Rankem. Distilled (D.I.) water was utilized all through the experiment.

Carbon Quantum dots (CQD) were prepared via an electrochemical method [28,29]. Briefly, two graphite rods (6 cm long & 0.5 cm width) were used as anode and cathode electrodes. These electrodes were immersed in 15 mL D.I. water and separated by a 5 mm distance. Then, a potential of 30 V and a current of 5 A were applied on these electrodes using a DC power supply. Subsequently, the colourless solution was changed to a dark brown solution in the reaction cell under continuous stirring for 10 h. The resultant CQD solution was collected and stored.

CQD modified Boron-doped Exfoliated g-C3N4 (CQD@B-ECN) was synthesized by ultrasound-assisted thermal condensation method as elucidated in Fig. S1. In brief, the required amount of pristine g-C3N4 (CN) from melamine and boric acid were dissolved in 10 mL isopropyl alcohol. The suspension was exposed to probe sonicator (20 kHz, 750 W, continuous mode) for 60 min, subsequently added x μL (x = 1–4) of prepared CQD. The homogenized mixture was heated to 70–80 °C under magnetic stirring to remove the solvent and the resulting complex crystal was granulated uniformly using mortar and pestle. The dehydrated powder was transferred into a ceramic crucible with a lid and maintained at 550 °C in a muffle furnace for 4 h (ramp rate: 5 °C/min). After calcination, the catalyst was cooled naturally to room temperature. Then, the resulting xCQD@yB-ECN material was collected and granulated to a fine powder. Under similar conditions, we synthesized yB-ECN (y = 1–4 wt%) without CQD, xCQD-ECN without boric acid, and ECN without both boric acid and CQD. The pure CN was prepared by thermal polymerization using melamine as a precursor at 550 °C for 4 h [30].

The Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) was carried out on the IRTracer-100-Shimadzu. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained by the Aeris-Malvern Panalytical X-ray Benchtop diffractometer. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on scanning XPS microprobe-PHI VersaProbe III. The morphology was observed via high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEM-2100Plus-JEOL) and high-resolution scanning electron microscopy (HR-SEM, Apreo S-Thermofisher). The multi-point Brunauer-Emmett-Teller specific surface area (BET SSA) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) pore size distribution were analysed by Autosorb iQ-quantachrome. The UV–vis diffused reflectance spectra (UV-DRS) was recorded using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-3600i Plus). A fluorescence spectrophotometer (Fluorolog-QM-Horiba) was used to examine the photoluminescence (PL) spectra. The electron spin resonance (ESR) was carried out on JES-FA200-JEOL ESR spectrometry (8.75–9.65 GHz of X-band frequency).

The Nyquist plots, transient photocurrent (TPC) response, and Mott-Schottky (M − S) plots of as-prepared catalysts were obtained using an electrochemical workstation (BioLogic SP-150). A standard three-electrode system contains sample-working electrode, Ag/AgCl-reference electrode and Pt-counter electrode in 0.1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte. The working electrode was fabricated as follows: 1 mg catalyst was uniformly dispersed in 5 μL of Nafion (diluted with 1:1 ratio of D.I. water and ethanol) using a bath ultrasonicator for 30 min to form a slurry. Then, 4 μL slurry was dropped on glassy carbon (GC) electrode (3 mm diameter) and dried at room temperature for 1 h. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) Nyquist plots were measured at 0.5 V with a frequency range of 100 kHz─100 mHz. For TPC response, AM1.5 solar simulator (300WSS-EM-Photo Emission Tech) was utilized as a light source. The M − S plots were obtained with a frequency range of 500 Hz─1 kHz.

TC degradation was performed under ultrasonic irradiator and visible light illuminator along with catalyst. The ultrasound irradiation was generated using an ultrasonic bath sonicator with a 40 kHz frequency and 600 W power for 25 L capacity (Labman Wensar). The water bath temperature in the ultrasonicator was maintained at 30 °C using ice cubes/packs addition. Besides, the visible light was illuminated from the 300 W Halogen lamp.

The sonophotocatalytic activity of the as-prepared catalysts was examined for TC degradation in aqueous solutions. 40 mg photocatalyst was dispersed in 40 mL TC solution (15 ppm). The reaction mixtures were stirred for 30 min in the dark condition to achieve the adsorption/desorption equilibrium status of photocatalyst and TC. Afterwards, the reaction solution was kept under an ultrasonic irradiator and visible light illuminator. The reaction solution of about 2.5 mL was collected at definite intervals and filtered with a Millipore filter (0.22 μm). The peak absorbance of TC at 357 nm was used to determine its concentration in the aqueous solution by UV–vis Spectrophotometer (Cary 5000-Agilent). For illustration purposes, photocatalytic and sonocatalytic activities were carried out individually for TC degradation. The amount of organic carbon was analysed by the total organic carbon analyser (TOC, TOC-L-Shimadzu). The reusability and stability of the as-prepared photocatalyst were evaluated after the sonophotocatalytic activity. The photocatalyst was collected through centrifugation and washed with D.I. water and ethanol followed by drying and reused for the consecutive cycle. The radical trapping studies were conducted by means of quenchers containing 1 mM of tert-butyl alcohol (TBA for •OH), benzoquinone (BQ for •O2 −), and Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA for h+).

The degradation intermediates during the reaction pathways of TC were identified by using liquid chromatography (Shimadzu, LCMS-2020) coupled with mass spectrometry (Agilent, 1260 infinity-HPLC). The sample was eluted by a C18 column (5 μm, 4.6 mm × 150 mm, Zorbax Eclipse plus) and detected at 276 nm and 357 nm by Shimadzu LabSolutions software. The mobile phase consists of 37% acetonitrile and 0.1% formic acid. The gradient elution was performed for 40 min with a 50 μL injection volume and 1 mL/min flow rate. The electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (ESI-MS) was operated in positive polarity. The mass range was from 50 to 1000 m/z.

The first-principles calculations are employed within the framework of DFT by projector-augmented wave pseudopotentials (PAW) method using the Quantum ESPRESSO [31]. The generalized gradient approximation of Perdew−Burke−Ernzerhof (PBE) is used for the exchange-correlation functional [32]. The plane-wave set is expanded within an energy cut-off of 400 eV. The 5 × 5 × 1 mesh of k points in the Monkhorst−Pack scheme are chosen for the Brillouin zone sampling for optimization, formation energy (element doping), and migration energy calculations. Energy optimization is considered when the magnitude of force per atom is smaller than 0.001 eV/Å. All crystal structures are illustrated using the VESTA visualization software.

Fig. 1 (a) represents the XRD pattern of CN, ECN, 2B-ECN, 2CQD-ECN and 2CQD-2B-ECN photocatalysts with their graphite stacking structures (JCPDS No. 87–1526). The characteristic diffraction peaks centered at 27.35° and 13.01° correspond to crystal planes (002) and (100), respectively. The strong diffraction peak at 27.35° relates inter-layer stacking of the conjugated aromatic system with 0.326 nm. The weak diffraction peak at 13.01° relates to in-plane packing of repeating tri-s-triazine units with 0.675 nm. For the 2B-ECN, the intensity of the (002) peak is decreased and shifted to 27.49° which indicates the delamination and increased layer disorder due to exfoliation (ECN) and boron incorporation into the crystal structure of CN through the replacement of C or N sites [33]. In the case of 2CQD-ECN and 2CQD-2B-ECN, the (002) peak intensity of CN is increased with CQD loading along with peak shift due to exfoliation and boron incorporation, respectively.

The FT-IR spectra were analysed to gain more insight and understanding of the functional groups for the prepared photocatalyst, as revealed in Fig. 1(b). The sharp peak at 807 cm−1 is attributed to the breathing mode of tri-s-heptazine or tri-s-triazine units. The strong band of 1200–1700 cm−1 with the characteristic peaks at 1202, 1230, 1317, 1397, 1455, 1538, and 1628 cm−1 corresponds to the C–N/C=N stretching vibrational mode. Besides, the broad peak between 2900 and 3400 cm−1 is associated with NH2/N–H stretching vibration and the O–H stretching vibration of the physically adsorbed H2O [34]. The CN characteristic peaks appeared in all CN, ECN, 2B-ECN, 2CQD-ECN and 2CQD-2B-ECN photocatalysts. However, the FT-IR spectra of ECN shows an increased intensity compared to CN, manifesting in a sharper tri-s-triazine units peak, broad C–N, C–O and strong N–H stretching vibration. The aforementioned observation indicates that the ultrasonic exfoliation of CN shows a strong interconnected bond between the CN network [35]. Furthermore, boron doping in ECN influences the vibration modes of C–N heterocycles, and the characteristic peaks of 2B-ECN become weaker than ECN. Moreover, the characteristics peak of the C–O stretching vibration at 1396 cm−1 is further enhanced upon uniform deposition of CQD on the ECN and 2B-ECN surface.

The surface morphologies and micro-structures of the as-prepared catalysts were scrutinized using HR-SEM analyses. As presented in Fig. 2 (a and b), bulk CN displays nanosheets with massive irregular aggregates stacked by a lamellar structure. After ultrasonication, the bulk CN exfoliated, which led to the formation of fragmental CN nanosheets. Moreover, the boron doping and CQD incorporation in ECN enhanced the porosity of the 2CQD-2B-ECN surface as depicted in Fig. 2(c and d). Besides, the elemental mapping of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and boron (B) in 2CQD-2B-ECN indicates the uniform distribution of B doping in ECN, while CN represents only C and N elements as depicted in Fig. S2. EDX spectra (Figs. S2(c and g)) and the corresponding atomic/weight % (Table S1) indicates the nitrogen-vacancy occurrence during boron doping followed by CQD incorporation in 2CQD-2B-ECN. The presence of aluminium (Al) can be attributed to the substrate that was used in the EDX study. Further, these results were analysed by HR-TEM and represented in Fig. 2(e–h). Typical HR-TEM results reveal that CQD is anchored onto the 2B-ECN nanosheets (Fig. 2(h)). These modifications can significantly strengthen the interfacial interaction between the π structure of CQD and 2B-ECN, thus promoting the separation of photo-induced charge carriers.

The BET SSA of the as-prepared catalysts were examined by nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms. The synthesized catalysts exposed type IV isotherms with H3 type hysteresis loops indicating mesoporous structure according to the IUPAC classification, as shown in Fig. 2(i). The BET SSA was estimated to be 73 m2/g for CN, 144 m2/g for ECN, 406 m2/g for 2B-ECN, 325 m2/g for 2CQD-ECN and 685 m2/g for 2CQD-2B-ECN (Table 1 ). The BET SSA of 2CQD-2B-ECN is significantly (9-fold) greater than that of CN, suggesting enhancement of surface area due to the boron doping induced nitrogen vacancies in ECN followed by CQD decoration. This is also evident from the DFT calculations (Fig. 5(b)). For the 2CQD-2B-ECN, substantial enlargement of the nano-porous can also be observed in the BJH curve (Fig. 2(i)-inset). The BJH pore size distribution of the 2CQD-2B-ECN is relatively broad signifying a large pore volume of 2.935 cm3/g when compared to CN (0.465 cm3/g), ECN (0.621 cm3/g), 2B-ECN (1.282 cm3/g), 2CQD-ECN (0.902 cm3/g) as observed in HR-SEM images (Fig. 2 (c)) of 2CQD-2B-ECN. A high BET SSA and BJH pore volume of the 2CQD-2B-ECN provide an abundance of active sites that facilitates the redox reaction with extensive contact between catalyst and TC. Thereby, it improves the charge transport and minimizes the recombination probability of photoexcited carriers.

The surface chemical composition and oxidation states for the prepared photocatalyst were investigated by XPS. The survey spectra indicate the presence of C1s, N1s and O1s for both catalysts, while the B1s peak only appears for 2CQD-2B-ECN (Fig. 3(a), S3(a-d)). The high-resolution C1s spectra de-convoluted into four peaks at binding energies of 283.2, 284.7, 286.6 and 287.3 eV (Fig. 3 (b)), ascribed to C–B, graphitic C=C/C–C, C–N/C=N and aromatic sp2-C in N=C–N, respectively. The N1s spectra in Fig. 3(c) is centered at 396.9 eV for sp2-N in C=N–C of tri-s-triazine rings, 398.2 eV for B atom incorporation in ECN (B–N), 400 eV for ternary N/interlinking sp3 bonded N–(C)3 and 403 eV for π-excitations cause charging effects or positive charge localization in heterocycles. The C1s and N1s spectra of 2CQD-2B-ECN had slightly shifted toward higher binding energy compared to the CN, which ascribed to the strong interfacial interaction between the CQD and B-ECN. Additionally, O1s spectra for 2CQD-2B-ECN (Fig. 3(d)) positioned at 530.5 and 531.5 eV are corresponds to surface chemisorbed hydroxyl groups and 1s–p∗ transition in the epoxide (C=O) structure due to surface modification and functionalization of CQD on 2B-ECN, which represents CQD resident carboxyl groups [36]. Moreover, the B1s spectra are identified for 2CQD-2B-ECN (Fig. 3(e)) with partial magnification where the atomic percentage of B atoms is 0.5%. The peak at around 190, 191.6 and 193.4 eV are attributed to the B–C, B–N, BC2N and B–O bonds, which illustrates the B existence in the CN lattice through substitution/interstitial doping on the C or N site. The N2 site substitutions were much more favorable for boron doping than the other N or C-site substitutions due to its lowest boron doping formation energy as evident from DFT calculations. The BC2N coordination suggests that B doping substitutes nitrogen in the aromatic ring [37]. The corresponding N/C atomic ratio of 2CQD-2B-ECN is lower than the CN signifying nitrogen-vacancy in 2CQD-2B-ECN (Table S2) which is also confirmed from EDX analysis. The DFT calculations reveal that the N3 site is thermodynamically most favorable to form the vacancy with the lowest nitrogen-vacancy formation. Consequently, the experimental findings reveal the CQD incorporation and successful B substitution in the tri-s-triazine network, which are responsible for nitrogen-vacancy in the system.

The optical characteristics of the as-prepared catalysts were evaluated by UV–vis DRS spectroscopy and the results are revealed in Fig. 4 (a). The CN shows strong absorption in the UV–visible region with an absorption edge at approximately ∼473 nm. Unlike CN, the absorption edge of 2B-ECN shows a significant blue shift from 473 to 467 nm in UV–vis DRS spectra. This phenomenon can be attributed to the change in polymerization degree and the π-plane conjugation degree of the CN structure [38]. These varied absorption edges of 2B-ECN confirm boron incorporation into the ECN structure and consequently modified the intrinsic electronic properties of ECN. Further, the introduction of low CQD content has improved the light absorbance of ECN and 2B-ECN to a certain extent in the visible light region through photon upconversion, resulting in superior light-harvesting properties. CQDs have conjugated complexes and oxygen-containing functional groups, as shown in Fig. S4 [39]. The bandgap energies are determined according to the Tauc plot equation as follow.
(1) (αhv)1/n = A(hν − Eg)
where, ‘α’-absorption coefficient, ‘h’-Planck's constant, ‘ν’-incident light frequency, ‘Eg’-bandgap energy, ‘A’-constant and ‘n’-variable dependence on the type of bandgap [40]. As revealed in (Fig. 4(b)), the bandgap value increased from ∼2.77 eV (CN) to 2.789 (ECN, 2CQD-ECN) and 2.83 eV (2B-ECN, 2CQD-2B-ECN), which can be attributed to the exfoliation and boron doping. Moreover, 2CQD-2B-ECN shows enhanced light absorption due to CQD incorporation which in turn enhances the sonophotocatalytic activity.

The PL spectra of the as-prepared catalysts were examined at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm to empathize with the photogenerated charge carrier separation/transfer process. It exhibits a strong intrinsic photo-emission band at 460 nm, relating to the optical bandgap of CN signifying the existence of π states. The PL emission peak is attributed to electron-hole (e−/h+) pair recombination in band-to-band transitions during the photogeneration [41,42]. As shown in Fig. 4(c), the PL spectra of 2CQD-2B-ECN < 2B-ECN < 2CQD-ECN < ECN revealed a weaker emission peak than CN. It is worth noting that the PL intensity of 2CQD-2B-ECN is dramatically lowered, signifying hindered radiative recombination and faster charge transfer. In addition, the photoelectrochemical characteristics of the as-prepared catalysts were examined using TPC response and EIS-Nyquist plots to interpret the charge migration performance. As depicted in Fig. 4(d), 2CQD-2B-ECN exhibits approximately two times higher photocurrent response upon light illumination in contrast to CN and other synthesized (ECN, 2B-ECN, 2CQD-ECN) catalysts. The TPC response of 2CQD-2B-ECN indicates a longer electron lifetime and excellent charge carrier separation efficiency as a result of CQD incorporation which effectively transfers and stores photo-generated electrons. Moreover, the arc radius of the EIS-Nyquist plot of 2CQD-2B-ECN is substantially less than that of 2B-ECN < 2CQD-ECN < ECN and CN (Fig. 4(e)), implying that 2CQD-2B-ECN has a minimal charge transfer resistance value. These findings indicate that boron doping with nitrogen-vacancy improves the ECN surface state, which is beneficial for sonophotocatalytic activity.

ESR spectroscopy was utilized to detect unpaired electrons in the synthesized catalysts. A single Lorentzian line with a g-value of 2.000 is observed for ECN, 2B-ECN, 2CQD-ECN and 2CQD-2B-ECN (Fig. 4(f)) in the applied magnetic field from 334 to 343 mT, which is associated with the unpaired delocalized electrons of sp2 carbon atoms within π-conjugated aromatic rings. The Lorentzian line intensity of 2CQD-2B-ECN < 2B-ECN < 2CQD-ECN < ECN are superior to CN, which signifies the higher number of unpaired electrons in the 2CQD-2B-ECN. Conversely, negligible Lorentzian line intensity is perceived in CN, confirming its appropriate structural regularity and few localized unpaired electrons. The boron effect in ECN is accountable for increased Lorentzian line intensity of 2CQD-2B-ECN compared to CQD loading on ECN, which leads to nitrogen-vacancy. When nitrogen-vacancy exists in the CN network, the unpaired electrons will be rapidly redistributed to their neighboring carbon atoms by the delocalized π conjugated aromatic ring of CN [43]. Sequentially, these effects are beneficial for the photo-generation of reactive free radicals for sonophotocatalytic TC degradation. Therefore, the existence of nitrogen-vacancies confined in 2CQD-2B-ECN has been confirmed using the XPS, ESR, and EDX data.

A 2 × 2 × 1 supercell of pure CN monolayer is created with lattice parameters of a = b = 14.27 Å, containing 24 C and 32 N atoms. A vacuum spacing of 15 Å in the c axis is presented to avoid interactions between layers, as depicted in Fig. 5. For boron doping, five substitutional positions are considered, such as C1, C2, N1, N2 and N3 (as labelled in Fig. 5(a)). The doping formation energies (

Table 2 illustrates the doping formation energies of the different B doped ECN systems. The positive values of all doping formation energies represent thermodynamically endothermic and unspontaneous. Among, the N2 site is most likely doping position for B due to its lower formation energy of about 0.08 eV [44].

ce:section.ce:para:

The sonocatalytic, photocatalytic, sonophotocatalytic activities of the as-prepared catalysts were assessed by TC degradation under ultrasonic irradiation and/or light illumination. As portrayed in Figs. S7(a–o), the sono-, photo-, sonophotocatalytic degradation of TC was evaluated using UV–vis absorption spectra. The intensity of the TC characteristic absorption band at 357 nm decreases with an increase in time. Fig. 6 represents the time-dependent variation in TC concentration (C/C0) for CN, ECN, 2B-ECN, 2CQD-ECN and 2CQD-2B-ECN subjected to sono-, photo- and sonophotocatalysis. The C/C0 plot, where ‘C’ and ‘C0’ represents the TC concentration after the relevant catalytic activity for ‘t’ time and the initial TC concentration, respectively [46]. Before the sono- and/or photocatalytic activity of TC, the samples were stirred to attain the adsorption/desorption equilibrium of TC in dark conditions (Fig. S5) [47–49]. The adsorption efficiency varied approximately 1–2% concerning CN, 2B-ECN and 2CQD-2B-ECN specific surface areas were consistent with the above-described BET, TEM and SEM.

As seen in Fig. 6(a), CN showed 0.2%, 8.3%, 20.1% for sono-, photo- and sonophotocatalytic TC degradation, respectively. This is mainly due to the strong interaction between the 2D CN layers and the lesser number of surface-active sites to carry the reaction over. Besides, the catalytic activity of CN is insufficient for TC degradation due to the high electron-hole pair (e−/h+) recombination rate as evidenced from PL studies. On the other hand, the exfoliation of CN (ECN) increased the photo- and sonophotocatalytic activity to 16.7% and 25.2%, respectively by increasing the specific surface area and exposing more reactive sites (Figs. 6(b), S8(a, b)). Subsequently, boron doping into the ECN creates nitrogen-vacancy sites on its surface, thereby improving the surface-active sites for the photocatalytic activity of CN as revealed in Figs. S8(c–h). The photocatalytic activity of the B-ECN is increased from 27.5% to 34.1% for boron doping proportion for 1–2 wt% into the ECN network. This increase can be attributed to the change in electron density and electronic structure by vacant p-orbital of boron and lone electron pairs of nitrogen [50]. Further, boron doping of about 3–4 wt% led to a decrease in photocatalytic activity from 21.1% to 12.2%. Therefore, excessive boron doping distorts the bonding angle and conduction path of ECN significantly. The photocatalytic degradation of TC using 2B-ECN is approximately 2.6 times greater than CN. Therefore, low boron doping on ECN retained the CN structure but modified the electronic properties significantly, indicating the nitrogen-vacancy existence as confirmed from EPR and XPS analysis [51,52]. Moreover, the sonophotocatalytic activity of 2B-ECN shows 39.9%, whereas the sonocatalytic activity remains negligible, as depicted in Fig. 6(c).

After CQD incorporation onto the optimized 2B-ECN improve their interaction and form a stable composite. Subsequently, the CQD efficiently transfer and store the photo-generated electrons from 2B-ECN, as well as broadens their light-harvesting ability, which further enhances the sonophotocatalytic efficiency [53,54]. Figs. S9(a–g) shows the photocatalytic performance of TC using CQD incorporation onto ECN and 2B-ECN. As for the CQD-2B-ECN, the TC degradation efficiency was observed to be increasing from 63.2% to 68.6% for 1–2 μL CQD volume, respectively. With increasing the CQD volume to 3–4 μL, the photocatalytic activity of the composite gradually decreases to 68.3% and 66.8%, respectively. When CQD contents are sufficiently high, the number of available active sites can decrease, and serve as electron–hole pair recombination sites resulting in lower photocatalytic activity. As a result, the optimum CQD content is determined to be 2 μL of 0.6 mg/ml on 2B-ECN (2CQD-2B-ECN) as CQD agglomeration is limited, and the surface reaction active sites are improved. Contrary to this, 2CQD-ECN exhibits only 32.04% and 35.5% degradation through photo- and sonophotocatalysis (Fig. 6(d)), which implies the significant role of boron doping in ECN. As boron doping increases the catalytic active sites and facilitates the strong adsorption of TC molecules, while the nitrogen-vacancy due to B-doping further enhances the surface area of the synthesized catalyst. On the other hand, CQD decoration prevents the recombination of charge carriers and enhances the rate of reaction. Furthermore, 2CQD-2B-ECN reveals negligible for sonocatalysis and 84.2% for sonophotocatalytic efficiency, as seen from Fig. 6(e). The sonophotocatalytic degradation of TC using 2CQD-2B-ECN is approximately 4.2 times greater than that of CN and 2.1 times that of 2B-ECN (Fig. 6(f)). The complete degradation of TC using 2CQD-2B-ECN was observed within 180 min (Fig. S6). The detailed discussion for the comparison between CN and ECN, ECN and CQD-ECN, ECN and B-ECN, and CQD-B-ECN and CQD-ECN/B-ECN has been provided in the SI. Discussion. Evidently, this enhanced catalytic activity of TC explained as aforementioned, boron atoms of 2CQD-2B-ECN could participate in the CN framework formation by substituting nitrogen atoms, while the CQD closely deposit on the surface of CN [55,56].

As seen in Fig. 6(a–e), the degradation efficiency of TC indicates the trend: sonophotocatalysis > photocatalysis > sonocatalysis. The synergistic impact of sonophotocatalytic degradation of TC is related to the combined processes of sonocatalysis and photocatalysis. Eq. (1) used to estimate the synergistic effect quantitatively:

The sonophotocatalytic degradation of TC shows a synergistic effect using 2CQD-2B-ECN with a synergy index of 1.2. This synergistic effect of sonophotocatalysis can be described by the following factors.
(i)
Ultrasound-induced de-aggregation increases the surface area of 2CQD-2B-ECN, facilitating TC mass transfer between the 2CQD-2B-ECN surface and the TC solution.
(ii)
Ultrasonication promotes the formation of reactive radicals.
(iii)
2CQD-2B-ECN surface could serve as a source of additional nuclei for the creation of bubbles, which in turn accelerates the TC degradation.

The stability and reusability of the 2CQD-2B-ECN were evaluated by successive cyclic sonophotocatalytic activity of TC as displayed in Fig. 7 (a–c). The 2CQD-2B-ECN still reveals high sonophotocatalytic activity even after five consecutive cycles without any noticeable decrease. Even though the photocatalyst surface adsorbs some TC molecules and/or by-products, these are knocked out (surface cleaning) through the microjet pitting caused by the ultrasonic cavitation. Thus, there won't be any delay in sonophotodegradation in the subsequent run, i.e., maintaining the rate of reaction from one run to the other [57–59]. Moreover, the FT-IR spectra and HR-SEM images also confirm the functional group and structure of 2CQD-2B-ECN remains almost unaffected even after five successive cycles of sonophotocatalytic activity. Further, the TOC removal efficiency of TC solution is 72.5%, demonstrating the sonophotocatalytic ability of 2CQD-2B-ECN for TC degradation (Fig. 7(d)). In order to identify the main oxidative species produced in the 2CQD-2B-ECN during sonophotocatalytic TC degradation, radical trapping studies were carried out by means of quenchers. Benzoquinone (BQ), EDTA, and tert-butyl alcohol (TBA) were used as quenchers for •O2 −, h+, and •OH, respectively. As seen in Fig. 7(e), the sonophotocatalytic degradation efficiency of TC drops dramatically in the presence of BQ, from 84% to 36%. When TBA is utilized in the sonophotocatalytic process, the degradation of TC is reduced to 51%. Despite this, the inclusion of EDTA into the reaction decreased the degradation of TC to 68%. These findings suggest that the most substantial reactive species for sonophotocatalytic processes are in the order of •O2 − > •OH > h+. In addition, the sonophotocatalytic activity is compared to previously reported catalysts (Table S3), demonstrating that 2CQD-2B-ECN might be a feasible catalyst for several refractory pollutant degradations.

LC–MS technique was used to investigate the preliminary degradation products of TC over 2CQD-2B-ECN. MS spectra shows (Fig. S10) the initial and sonophotocatalytic treated TC solution for 120 min. Based on the potential configurations of intermediate products concerning charge-to-mass ratio (m/z), the proposed sonophotocatalytic degradation pathway of TC is depicted in Scheme 1 . Concisely, TC (m/z = 445) transformed to TC 1 (m/z = 419) and TC 2 (m/z = 320) via de-methylation reaction, cleavage of the carbo-atomic ring (de-cyclization) and de-amination. TC 2 fragmented to TC 3 (m/z = 306) and TC 4 (m/z = 279) through de-hydroxylation and de-carbonylation, respectively. Then, TC 4 is transformed to TC 5 (m/z = 236), TC 6 (m/z = 223) and TC 7 (m/z = 212) through cyclic ester formation, ring-opening (de-cyclization) and de-methylation processes. These intermediates are further degraded to generate ring-opening products TC 8 (m/z = 196) and finally oxidized into H2O and CO2 [60].

To ensure the semiconductor nature and band edge potential, the electronic band-structure of CN and 2CQD-2B-ECN were investigated using M − S plots. As revealed in Fig. 8 (a and b), the positive slopes of CN and 2CQD-2B-ECN at frequencies ranging from 500 Hz to 1000 Hz indicate characteristic n-type semiconductors. Based on these plots, flat band (fb) potentials were assessed to be 1.426 V for CN and 1.39 V for 2CQD-2B-ECN vs. Ag/AgCl. Consequently, their conduction band minimum (CBM) can be evaluated by the following Eq. (3).
(6) Efb (vs NHE) = Efb (vs. Ag/AgCl) + (0.059∗pH) + E(AgCl)
where E(AgCl) is 0.197 V and pH of Na2SO4 electrolyte is 7 at 25 °C. As a result, the CBM was estimated to be −0.81 V for CN and −0.78 V for 2CQD-2B-ECN vs. NHE. Further, the valence band maximum (VBM) was calculated using Eq. (4) and determined to be 1.96 V for CN and 2.05 V for 2CQD-2B-ECN vs. NHE.
(7) EVB = Eg + ECB

According to the estimated bandgap energies and band edge potentials, the detailed electronic band structures of the CN and 2CQD-2B-ECN were presented in Fig. 8(c). The N 2p state dominates the VBM, whereas the CBM is predominantly derived from the hybridization of the C 2p and N 2p states of CN [61]. Evidently, boron atoms of 2CQD-2B-ECN could participate in CN framework formation by substituting nitrogen atoms and inducing nitrogen vacancies, while the CQD closely deposit on the surface of CN. These results indicate the strong interaction between the conjugated π structure of CQD and B-ECN, which effectively downshift the VBM and CBM. Therefore, the positive shift of 2CQD-2B-ECN has a suitable band structure compared with CN for the sonophotocatalytic TC degradation.

Based on the discussions, a plausible sonophotocatalytic TC degradation mechanism was proposed using 2CQD-2B-ECN, as shown in Scheme 2 . The cavitation bubbles with enormous temperature and pressure produce extremely reactive radicals including hydroxyl ( • OH), hydrogen ( • H), hydroperoxyl ( • HOO) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) during implosion as per Eqs. (8)–(10).
(8) H2O + ))) → • H + • OH
(9) H2O + • OH → H2O2 + • H
(10) H2O2 + • H → H2 + HOO •

These reactive radicals assist in the destruction of TC. Besides, the presence of 2CQD-2B-ECN serve as additional nuclei for the cavitation bubbles creation and increases the reactive radical generation, thereby accelerating the TC degradation. Furthermore, the bubble implosion may emit light (sonoluminescence), which may excite the 2CQD-2B-ECN to form photogenerated electron-hole pairs. However, the sonoluminescence effect could be extremely weak [62]. Subsequently, under visible light illumination, CN excites to form the photogenerated electron and hole on the CB and VB as following Eq. (11)–(14), respectively.
(11) 2CQD-2B-ECN + hʋ → h+ + e−
(12) h+ + OH → • OH
(13) e− + O2 → •O2 −
(14) (h+, • OH, •O2 −) + TC →H2O + CO2

The photogenerated charge carrier separation can be accelerated by the coordination of lone pair electron in nitrogen with boron atom, which can delocalize π conjugated aromatic ring of CN with nitrogen-vacancies. Moreover, nitrogen-vacancy at the N3 site (DFT calculation) in B-ECN makes it the most active site with three unpaired electrons (the strong ESR signal observed for 2CQD-2B-ECN) and also provides a significantly larger reactive surface (higher BET SSA 685 m2/g) for the large TC molecules to be adsorbed without distorting the system. Subsequently, CQD incorporation enhances light-harvesting ability through the photon upconversion process and also significantly strengthens the interfacial interaction between the π structure of CQD and 2B-ECN, thus further promoting the photogenerated charge carrier separation. The photogenerated charge carriers migrate to the surface of 2CQD-2B-ECN in which h+ oxidizes the adsorbed H2O to • OH and e− reduces the O2 to •O2 −, which degrade the adsorbed TC. Further, ultrasound-induced de-aggregation increases the surface area of 2CQD-2B-ECN, facilitating TC mass transfer between the 2CQD-2B-ECN surface and the TC solution. Therefore, sonophotocatalytic TC degradation using 2CQD-2B-ECN leads to a synergistic impact ascribed to the combination of sonocatalysis and photocatalysis.

In summary, we successfully prepared CQD-B-ECN via ultrasound-assisted thermal condensation. The crystalline nature and functional groups of the as-prepared catalysts were confirmed by XRD and FT-IR analysis. HR-SEM and HR-TEM revealed the layered stacking CN exfoliated into fragmental CN nanosheets along with uniform distribution of boron and CQD. The BET SSA of 2CQD-2B-ECN was significantly greater than that of CN, suggesting abundant active sites to facilitate the redox reaction. Consequently, boron atoms actively participated in the ECN framework formation by substituting nitrogen atoms and inducing nitrogen-vacancies as evident from DFT calculations, XPS and EPR. Thereby, the recombination rate of photo-generated e−-h+ pairs is minimized by an improved charge transfer mechanism as explained from PL, photocurrent and EIS studies. Besides, CQD incorporation enhanced the light-harvesting ability of 2B-ECN as observed from UV–Vis studies. The downshifted VBM and CBM of 2CQD-2B-ECN generated a suitable band structure for TC degradation analysed by M − S plots. Subsequently, 2CQD-2B-ECN remarkably enhanced the synergistic effect of sonophotocatalytic TC degradation when compared to 2B-ECN and CN. Moreover, the complete TC removal was achieved within 180 min using 2CQD-2B-ECN and the TOC removal efficiency was 72.5% in 120 min. Based on these findings, a plausible mechanism and sonophotocatalytic TC degradation pathway over 2CQD-2B-ECN has been proposed, in which reactive radicals mineralised TC molecules to produce H2O and CO2. This work reveals that highly stable 2CQD-2B-ECN as to be a promising catalyst for diverse refractory pollutant removal via sonophotocatalysis.

Mani Preeyanghaa: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Vasudevan Vinesh: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation. Ponnambalam Sabarikirishwaran: Formal analysis. Anand Rajkamal: Formal analysis. Muthupandian Ashokkumar: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Bernaurdshaw Neppolian: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Resources.
